In this thesis, we developed procedures for constructing joint confidence regions for both the ranks (Algorithms \ref{alg:rank_asymp} and \ref{alg:rank_secondlevelbs}) and the ranked parameters (Algorithm \ref{alg:nonrank_ci}) in settings where the estimates to be ranked may be correlated. The performance of the proposed approaches was evaluated under varying levels of parameter variability $V(\theta)$ (low, moderate, and high), different numbers of ranked parameters $K$, and two correlation structures with multiple correlation levels.

For the ranked parameters, we compared the joint confidence regions obtained from Algorithm \ref{alg:nonrank_ci} with `r IND$SHORTNAME` and `r BONF$SHORTNAME`. Results show that the proposed method is able to maintain coverage close to the nominal level while producing tighter joint confidence regions in the presence of moderate to high correlation. This advantage is particularly evident when ranking a larger number of estimates with low underlying parameter variability.

For the ranks, Algorithm \ref{alg:rank_asymp} was found to systematically produce anti-conservative joint confidence regions, especially under high parameter variability. In addition, it tends to yield wider regions compared to Algorithm \ref{alg:rank_secondlevelbs}, making the latter a more reliable approach in practice.

The applicability of the proposed methods was demonstrated using the Pulse Asia and mean travel time to work datasets. The resulting joint confidence regions for the ranked parameters were consistent with the patterns observed in the simulation study, reinforcing the practical relevance of the findings.

Several limitations of the study are acknowledged. Algorithm \ref{alg:rank_asymp} may produce a negative difference in step 3, as observed in the first example, which limits its applicability to cases when the estimates are close to zero. This issue did not arise in the simulation study, as the true parameter values were centered at higher means. Future work may focus on addressing this limitation and improving the robustness of the method. Additionally, further calibration may be explored to achieve coverage even closer to the nominal level. Moreover, this study assumes known correlation structures and levels. An important extension would be to incorporate the estimation of correlation directly from the data. Finally, the margin of errors were held constant across parameter settings; future research may investigate how varying them could potentially affect  the resulting joint confidence regions.
